coredata.dc:title: Heteroatom-engineered multicolor lignin carbon dots enabling bimodal fluorescent off-on detection of metal-ions and glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Pengfei
Xu, Jikun
Hou, Xinyan
Dai, Lin
Zhang, Jiaming
Xiao, Xiao
Huo, Kaifu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising subclass of optical nanomaterials with versatile functions in multimodal biosensing. Howbeit the rapid, reliable and reproducible fabrication of multicolor CDs from renewable lignin with unique groups (e.g., –OCH3, –OH and –COOH) and alterable moieties (e.g., β-O-4, phenylpropanoid structure) remains challenging due to difficult-to-control molecular behavior. Herein we proposed a scalable acid-reagent strategy to engineer a family of heteroatom-doped multicolor lignin carbon dots (LCDs) that are functioned as the bimodal fluorescent off-on sensing of metal-ions and glutathione (GSH). Benefiting from the modifiable photophysical structure via heteroatom-doping (N, S, W, P and B), the multicolor LCDs (blue, green and yellow) with a controllable size distribution of 2.06–2.22 nm deliver the sensing competences to fluorometric probing the distinctive metal-ion systems (Fe3+, Al3+ and Cu2+) under a broad response interval (0–500 μM) with excellent sensitivity and limit of detection (LOD, 0.45–3.90 μM). Meanwhile, we found that the addition of GSH can efficiently restore the fluorescence of LCDs by forming a stable Fe3+-GSH complex with a LOD of 0.97 μM. This work not only sheds light on evolving lignin macromolecular interactions with tunable luminescent properties, but also provides a facile approach to synthesize multicolor CDs with advanced functionalities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor lignin carbon dots
Heteroatom-doping
Bimodal off-on sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rational design of multicolor carbon dots by well-defined lignin structure
Physicochemical properties of heteroatom-doped luminescent lignin carbon dots
Bimodal fluorescence sensing performance of lignin carbon dots with metal-ions and glutathione

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 22008159), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project (2022020801020312), Huanggang Wellman Biosciences Co. Ltd., and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018T110770).

The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32171728 and 22008159 ), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project ( 2022020801020312 ), Huanggang Wellman Biosciences Co., Ltd. , and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018T110770 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic roadmap of the multicolor-emissive lignin carbon dots (LCDs) using industrial lignin as the sole precursor. (a) An acid-engineering strategy for preparing blue, green, and yellow fluorescent LCDs from sulfuric acid (SCDs), tungstic acid (WCDs), phosphoric acid (PCDs), and boric acid (BCDs) respectively. As a reference, the HCDs represent the hydrothermally prepared LCDs with the absence of mineral acids. (b) Quantitative functional groups (mmol g−1) of hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxyl (−COOH) in lignin. CS: condensed syringyl unit. CG: condensed guaiacyl unit. Total P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyl. (c) The weight- average (M w) and number-average (M n) molecular weights, and polydispersity index (PDI, M w/M n) of lignin. Basic structural linkages of lignin including (d) side-chain δC/δ H 50–90/2.5–6.0 and (e) aromatic regions δC/δ H 100–135/5.5–7.5 in the two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) spectrum. (f) Existent substructures of lignin identified in the 2D HSQC NMR spectra including β-aryl ether (A, β-O-4′ linkages with a free –OH at the γ‑carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′ substructures (A′), resinol (B, formed by β–β′, α-O-γ′, and γ-O-α′ linkages), phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β-5′ and α-O-4′ linkages), syringyl (S), oxidized syringyl (S′), ferulate (FA), guaiacyl, (G), and p-hydroxyphenyl units (H), free p-coumaric acid (pCA). (g) The potential scheme of the formation mechanism from lignin to multicolor CDs with the acidic heteroatom dopants.

TEM images of the as-prepared (a) WCDs, (b) BCDs and (c) HCDs (Inset panels deal with their bar-diagrams of nanoparticle size distribution measured by TEM). The corresponding high-resolution TEM images for determining their lattice size d of the (d) WCDs and (e) HCDs. AFM images and corresponding height profiles of the (f) WCDs, (g) BCDs, and (h) HCDs (insert: the height distribution curves). (i) Zeta potential (ζ) of the as-synthesized RCDs. (j) FT-IR spectra of the SCDs, WCDs and HCDs.

Full XPS survey scan of the selected (a) WCDs, (b) BCDs, and (c) HCDs. High-resolution XPS N 1s (d), W 4f (e), and S 2p (f) of the WCDs. High-resolution XPS N 1s (g), B 1s (h), and S 2p (i) of the BCDs.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (excitation E x and emission E m wavelengths) spectra of (a) SCDs, (b) WCDs, (c) PCDs, (d) BCDs, and (e) HCDs. Photoluminescence (PL) mapping images of (f) SCDs, (g) WCDs, (h) PCDs, and (i) BCDs excited at the varied wavelengths. PL mapping pattern (j) and fluorescence emission spectra (k) of HCDs excited at different wavelengths. (l) The time-resolved PL decay curves of all the lignin-derived CDs.

The fluorescent sensing selectivity of the SCDs and WCDs. Relative fluorescence emission intensities of the SCDs (a) and WCDs (b) upon adding various kinds of metal ions. Changes in SCDs fluorescence intensity upon adding different concentrations (0–500 μM) of Fe3+ (c) and the corresponding fitting plot of (F 0 − F)/F 0 (d). The changes of fluorescence spectra (e) of SCDs/Fe3+/glutathione system with the GSH concentration from 0 to 300 μM and their relationships (f). Emission spectra of WCDs with varying Fe3+ (g)/Al3+ (i) concentrations, and the fitting relationship between fluorescence quenching or reinforcing with the increased concentrations of Fe3+ (h)/Al3+ (j). The recovery of fluorescence spectra (k) of WCDs/Fe3+/GSH system with the gradually increased GSH concentration and the relationship (l) between intensity enhancement and GSH concentration.

Relative fluorescence emission intensities of the PCDs (a), BCDs (b), and HCDs (c) upon adding various kinds of metal ions. Changes in BCDs fluorescence intensity upon adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (d)/Cu2+ (f) and the corresponding fitting plot of Fe3+ (e)/Cu2+ (g). Emission spectra of HCDs with varying Fe3+ (h)/Cu2+ (j) concentrations, and the fitting relationship between fluorescence changes with the increased concentrations of Fe3+ (i)/Al3+ (k).

head.ce:title: Heteroatom-engineered multicolor lignin carbon dots enabling bimodal fluorescent off-on detection of metal-ions and glutathione

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising subclass of optical nanomaterials with versatile functions in multimodal biosensing. Howbeit the rapid, reliable and reproducible fabrication of multicolor CDs from renewable lignin with unique groups (e.g., –OCH3, –OH and –COOH) and alterable moieties (e.g., β-O-4, phenylpropanoid structure) remains challenging due to difficult-to-control molecular behavior. Herein we proposed a scalable acid-reagent strategy to engineer a family of heteroatom-doped multicolor lignin carbon dots (LCDs) that are functioned as the bimodal fluorescent off-on sensing of metal-ions and glutathione (GSH). Benefiting from the modifiable photophysical structure via heteroatom-doping (N, S, W, P and B), the multicolor LCDs (blue, green and yellow) with a controllable size distribution of 2.06–2.22 nm deliver the sensing competences to fluorometric probing the distinctive metal-ion systems (Fe3+, Al3+ and Cu2+) under a broad response interval (0–500 μM) with excellent sensitivity and limit of detection (LOD, 0.45–3.90 μM). Meanwhile, we found that the addition of GSH can efficiently restore the fluorescence of LCDs by forming a stable Fe3+-GSH complex with a LOD of 0.97 μM. This work not only sheds light on evolving lignin macromolecular interactions with tunable luminescent properties, but also provides a facile approach to synthesize multicolor CDs with advanced functionalities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a burgeoning subclass of luminescent nanoparticles consisting of carbonaceous structures with multifarious functional groups, low-toxic carbon dots (CDs) have been widely employed in fluorescent sensing [1,2], biological labeling [3,4], drug delivery, and light-emitting diodes due to their unique optical properties and facile preparing adaptability [5–8]. While extensive studies have been conducted in boosting the fluorescent quantum yield (QY) via manipulating the physicochemical oligomer structure, the multicolor characteristics of CDs from renewable biomass is largely overlooked, resulting in the unsatisfactory performance to boost the sustainable development [9–11]. To this end, the heteroatom-doping tactics (e.g., N, P, S and B) can serve as backups to prepare multicolor fluorescence CDs decorating with new functional sites. Hence exploring the facile and tunable acid-reagent engineering is of vital importance to popularize a fully integrated platform for scalable heteroatom-doping and preparing of CDs.

A wide variety of carbonaceous sources that include the carbon fiber, graphene, vitamins, arenes and the like are widely used to facilely prepare luminescent CDs [12,13]. Available as the most abundant, renewable aromatic macromolecules rich in β-O-4 bonds, lignin has always been counted as a scientific hotspot in the domain of biomass engineering that converts it into high-value chemicals and/or materials [14–16]. Encouragingly, lignin has been attested to be one of the perfect precursor with the ability in designing CDs, due to its unique aromatic conjugated structure and high carbon content (∼60 %) [17,18]. Over the past decades, the natural lignin irresistibly becomes an alternative to synthesizing CDs because of its heterogeneous phenolic structure (e.g., S, G and H referring to the syringyl, guaiacyl, and p-hydroxyphenyl units) and plentiful functional groups (e.g., −OH, −COOH, and –OCH3), providing the assembly of fundamental carbon skeleton (e.g., sp
2 /sp
3 hybridization) and surface modifications (e.g., defect and edge). For instance, nitrogen-doped lignin carbon dots (NLCDs) were fabricated as the carriers of Pt single atoms, the ternary Pt-NLCDs@CdS offered an exceptional visible-light-powered hydrogen evolution up to ∼46.10 mmol h−1 g−1 [19]. Nitrogen and lanthanum co-doped LCDs were synthetized from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin via an easily hydrothermal processing to synchronously detect Fe3+ and Sn2+ [20]. A novel N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC composite was fabricated through loading N, S-doped LCDs, performing in the efficient photo-degradation of Congo-red over a broad pH range from 3 to 11 [21]. Despite recent progress in designing luminescent LCDs, the situation of inefficient functionality, single near-blue emission, and unclear molecular evolution impede their large-scale applications. For the multipoint bridging of lignin molecules and CDs, a dilemma about preparing the multicolor LCDs with tunable heteroatom structure need to be tackled urgently in the trend of developing practical lignin-to-CDs proceeds.

In this work, we synthesized polychromatic fluorescent lignin carbon dots (LCDs) with heteroatom-doping from bioethanol tailings (supporting information S1.1) through a tunable acid-reagent engineering (e.g., sulfuric acid H2SO4, tungstic acid H2WO4, phosphoric acid H3PO4, and boric acid H3BO3). In order to grasp the basic understanding of luminescence mechanism, the structural characteristics of multicolor LCDs are linked with the tendency of lignin functional moieties to effectively navigate their PL sensing. A universally co-existing and complexes of heavy metals and organic pollutants in water have caused the inevitable damage to both environment and humans [22]. Afterwards, the LCDs function as the fluorescent probe with specific responding behavior to metal-ions (Fe3+, Cu2+ and Al3+) that also exhibits the acceptable selectivity and sensitivity. Besides, the quenching (Fe3+/Cu2+) and/or intensifying (Al3+) fluorescence of the LCD-ions mixed system can be restored in the presence of glutathione (GSH). To advance the practical lignin application, it is witnessed that a steerable acid-reagent engineering are efficiently shaped to produce high-performance multicolor LCDs with heteroatom-doping on a large-scale, preferably in a sustainable avenue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational design of multicolor carbon dots by well-defined lignin structure
ce:section.ce:para:
The superiority of this work can be elaborated as the following aspects: (1) we innovatively integrate the facile acid-reagent engineering with lignin-inspired CDs preparation to offset the gap between heterogeneous lignin fractions with multicolor-emissive, heteroatom-doped LCDs; (2) the unique multicolor emission of LCDs that originates from the quantum size, sp 2/sp 3 hybridization and surface functionality with the aid of tunable acid-reagents can navigate their selective recognition and “off-on” responsive sensitivity of metal-ions and GSH. The schematic diagram of synthesizing multicolor LCDs through a two-step strategy that includes nitric acid oxidization and inorganic acid cleavage/doping is explicitly interpreted in Fig. 1a. The oxidized lignin molecules with N-doped functionality can be transformed into the species of SCDs, WCDs, PCDs and BCDs through acid-reagent engineering about H2SO4, H2WO4, H3PO4 and H3BO3, respectively. Meanwhile, the N-doped lignin was directly treated via hydrothermal processing with the absence of acid to acquire HCDs. Biological lignin macromolecule hold unique aromatic structure that includes tunable G/S/H subunits and intrinsic functional groups, exhibiting a potential correlation with the ornament of sp
2 /sp
3 hybridization and defective carbon within CDs. Besides, it is of great concern to maneuver lignin molecular weight that influences the nanoparticle-size and photophysical state of CDs. Given all that, a congenerous importing of carbonaceous scaffold and surface functionality into LCDs could be implicated with the discrepancies of lignin fractions in the interior linkages, composition and reactivity. Hence explaining the unambiguous lignin structure is of crucial importance to link the physicochemical properties with fluorescent discriminating function of LCDs.

The quantitative 31P NMR technique is used for calculating the hydroxyls and carboxyls of lignin. As shown in Fig. 1b, the contents of aliphatic −OH, phenolic −OH, and − COOH in lignin molecules are quantified to be 1.483, 2.703, and 1.594 mmol g−1 respectively. The main species of phenolic −OH is G-OH (1.201 mmol g−1), followed by S-OH (0.589 mmol g−1) and H-OH (0.560 mmol g−1). Generally, the breaking of the β-O-4 linkages results in the exposure of phenolic −OH, and the oxidation reaction leads to the lignin cleavage with the newly formed carboxyls. This indicates that the occurrence of initial lignin depolymerization using HNO3 offers the potential to transfer more active sites to LCDs, such as carboxyl and phenolic −OH groups. The presence of condensed moieties of CG-OH (0.196 mmol g−1) and CS-OH (0.196 and 0.157 mmol g−1), illustrating the sectional condensation of lignin structure. As a potential consequence of average molecular weight, the distribution of phenolic structure from lignin macromolecules establishes a direct link with the fundamental carbons of LCDs, potentially responsible for the photophysical properties. As can be seen from Fig. 1c, the possible hybridization and nucleation boundaries of carbon cores are partially resolved by the weight-average (M w, 2350 g mol−1) and number-average (M n, 690 g mol−1) molecular weights of lignin. The calculated polydispersity index (PDI, M w/M n) of 3.41 indicates the dissimilar molecular or gradient scattering of heterogeneous lignin molecules, which provides the chance to produce polychromatic LCDs with various nano-sizes and optics.

The purpose of conducting 2D HSQC NMR technique is to explore an in-depth information of the structure and motifs of lignin (e.g., representative subunits and interior linkages). The branched chains of lignin with major interunit bonding are clearly discovered from the aliphatic region between δC/δ H 50–90/2.5–6.0 in Fig. 1d. The abundant β-aryl ether substructures (β-O-4′, A) are presented in the lignin fragments accompanied by the β-β (B, resinol) and β-5 (C, phenylcoumaran) linkages, [23]. It has been affirmed that the crystalline structure of lignin-derived carbon exhibits a positive linear correlation with the lignin β-O-4 bond [24]. In this context, the rational design of chemically manufacturing luminescent LCDs can be adjusted upon the guidance of lignin molecular substructures [24]. In the lignin side-chain, the inserted carbohydrates also have the ability to affect the disordered structure and sp
3 hybridization of LCDs through locating CH correlations in β-D-xylopyranoside at the aliphatic domain. As illustrated in Fig. 1e, the cross-linked signals in aromatic region ranging from 100 to 135/5.5–7.5 ppm belong to the fundamental phenylpropanoid substructures of lignin. We can easily locate the apparent cross-signals of syringyl (S), Cα‑carbonyl-oxidized syringyl (S′), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), ferulate (FA) and p-coumaric acid (pCA) [25]. From a branching perspective, specific S/G ratio of aromatic lignin hydrocarbons are prone to interact with methoxy groups, which might contribute to form edge structural defects through solvothermal demethoxylation. The acylation of C-γ from lignin side-chain offer the prominent signals of pCA, bringing a straight-forward proof to the leftover pCA during the industrial extraction procedure [26]. The related assignments of 13C 1H cross-signals about cardinal lignin motifs are demonstrated in Fig. 1f. More intriguingly, the potential oxidation of inherent G and/or S subunits of lignin might produce deuterogenous acids as aromatic chromophores, which are then in-situ confined by fatty acids with a hydrogen-bonding network, leading to enhanced spin-orbit coupling and luminescence [27]. Therefore, the oxidized lignin fragments with aliphatic –OH and –COOH create a possible opportunity to reinforce the fluorescence of LCDs by adhering oligomers on their surface with a molecular-state luminescence mechanism. Note that aliphatic and phenolic –OH are usually located in the side-chains and aromatic C-3 position of lignin respectively. From this point of view, varying the species and contents of −OH and/or –COOH in lignin subunits (e.g., S/G/H and their condensed moieties) holds a direct correlation to maneuver the fundamental carbon, defect structures, and functional species of LCDs, which affects the quenching and/or reinforcing phenomenon in metal-ions/organics systems.

In view of the above structure facts, a possible formation mechanism of LCDs that mainly includes acidic lignin dissociation and aromatic intermediate reconstitution to start-up nucleation is proposed as illustrated in Fig. 1g. It is widely believed that the main β-O-4 ether accounts for >50 % of the total lignin linkage bonds [28,29]. On this account, the oxidized splitting of lignin using ultrasonic HNO3 treatment enables in-situ introduction of sulfur into lignin segments along with N dopants by fracturing aryl-ether bonds (e.g., β-O-4′) and/or C − C linkages (e.g., β–5′). Thereinto, the tri-functional nitric acid allows a string of “top-down” scissors, molecular oxidizing, and N-doping agents to generate lignin fragments, thereby providing the intermediates to synthesize multicolor LCDs. Afterwards, the aromatic refusion and nucleation of heterogeneous lignin fragments could occur by means of the prompt sp
2 hybridization and π-π stacking during the acid-mediated solvothermal procedure. It is worth noting that several new active sites and functional groups of LCDs can be formed via a second-step acid-reagent engineering, which can introduce B-, W-, P-, and S-species into the N-doped LCDs. Hence, the tunable natures of vacancies, heteroatom-doping, geometric and electronic structure creates an opportunity to update the multicolor luminescence LCDs. Due to the differential lignin fragments, the manipulation of acid-reagent engineering triggers the variations in physicochemical properties of LCDs that jointly direct their multicolor emission to broaden the sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of heteroatom-doped luminescent lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
It is universally known that the fundamental characteristics of CDs determine their ultimate optical and/or sensing performance. Hence it is highly indispensable to employ transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) to survey the pattern and nanosize of LCDs. The TEM image shows that the as-prepared WCDs, BCDs, and HCDs belong to uniformly dispersed, quasi-spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 2a–c). From the insets, the average lateral sizes with a gradually increased trend are about 2.06 nm, 2.10 nm and 2.22 nm for WCDs, HCDs, and BCDs, respectively. It is generally appreciated that the photoluminescence (PL) properties varied with particle size. The red-shift of emission wavelength with incremental particle size refers to the decreasing band-gap caused by quantum confined π-electron delocalization effect [30]. Besides, the heteroatom-doping and surface modification might affect the luminescence of LCDs by controlling their defective structure and energy-level [31–33]. Except for the aforementioned situations, the alterable sp
2 /sp
3 hybridized domains are also related to luminescence. With regard to LCDs, the congenital sp
2 substructures of aromatic conjugated lignin motifs would furnish a foundation for transmitting the hybridizing sp
2 carbons into the skeleton. On the other side, the aliphatic side-chain from lignin provides the LCDs with sp 3 hybridized carbon. Under the circumstance of LCDs with comparable nanosize, the strengthened ratio of internal sp
2 hybridization leads to the red-shift phenomenon, revealing that the fluorescent emission wavelengths of LCDs are highly dependent on both the space-confined size and hybridized sp
2 /sp
3 domains [34]. From Fig. 2d–e, the high-resolution TEM images display the in-plane lattice d-spacing of graphene-like in HCDs and WCDs. The crystal inter-planar d-spacings of 0.21 and 0.34 nm correlate with the (100) and (002) planes of graphite respectively, which is probably assigned to the differences in solvent polarity, acidity and heteroatoms [35]. The AFM image reveals that LCDs have the topographic heights in the range of 0.25–2.5 nm, disclosing that most of the LCDs consist of 1–3 graphene-like assembly layers (Fig. 2f–h) [36]. The 3D profile diagram also affirms the uniform size distribution of as-prepared LCDs [37,38]. As depicted in Fig. 2i, the zeta potentials of LCDs are varied from −65.43 to 22.35 mV providing potential information of functional groups and water dispersity, which are ascribed to the intervention of heteroatom functionalization to adjust their surface charge via a facile acid-reagent engineering. [39]. According to this inference, the incidental charge intensities from LCDs are closely aligned with their unique abilities to chelate metal-ions. An integrated status of the particle size, oxidation degree, sp
2 /sp
3 hybridization and heteroatom-interrelated (e.g., W, P, B, S and N) functionality/ζ potentials of the as-prepared LCDs would change their luminescence. The QYs of as-prepared LCDs dissolved in neutral aqueous solution (pH = 7) are approximately 6.2–8.4 %. It should be mentioned that the QY of HCDs is slightly lower than that of SCDs, WCDs, PCDs and BCDs, disclosing a fact that the luminescent center might be destroyed due to extreme carbonization without the potential surface passivation via moderate acids.

The essential groups and elemental configuration of the selected LCDs are evaluated through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As illustrated in Fig. 2j, the strong peaks at 3426 cm−1 in FTIR spectra are originated from the stretching vibration of hydroxyls. The in-plane and out-of-plane bending of aromatic CH are located at 2936 and 1033 cm−1. The peaks at 1593 and 1340 cm−1 are ascribed to CC, and CN, respectively [40]. Due to the stretching vibrations of WO bond, the spectrum of WCDs indicates a band in the frequency range of 780–795 cm−1 [41]. The peaks located at 1152 and 628 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibration of CS [40,42]. The FTIR results potentially confirm that the acid-reagent engineering holds the ability of shaping heteroatom-contained functional groups into the carbon skeleton of LCDs via element-doping. The WCDs, BCDs, and HCDs were selected to study the condition of surface oxidation and chemical linkages with XPS analysis. The full-survey spectra of WCDs, BCDs and HCDs are presented in Fig. 3a–c. From Fig. 3a, the prominent peaks at ∼37.0, 168.0, 284.8, 399.1, and 532.1 eV are assigned to W4f, S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively. The spectrum of BCDs in Fig. 3b shows the particular peak of B1s at ∼193.0 eV, except for C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p respectively. With the absence of acid-reagent, the peaks of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s in HCDs are located at ∼168.0, 284.8, 399.1, and 532.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c). As displayed in Fig. 3d, four components of –NO2, NH, N–(C)3, and C–N–C split from the normalized N1s signal are fitted at 405.1 eV, 401.5 eV, 400.7 eV, and 399.2 eV respectively. We also can observe the CN, C-O/C=O, –NO2 motifs from the fitting curves of C1s and O1s for WCDs, BCDs and HCDs, respectively (Fig. S1 and S2). It can be inferred that nitrogen-dopants have a preference at the edges of the selected LCDs, which is attributed to their high crystallized structure with small defects [43]. The representative signals of W4f2/7(WO3) and W4f2/5(WO3) are also distributed at ∼36.0 and 38.0 eV from W4f fitting profile, respectively (Fig. 3e). The presence of C-S-C covalent bonds and C-SOx-C sulfone bridges can be well-fitted into three peaks at ∼168.1, 168.9, and 169.8 eV from the S2p pattern (Fig. 3f) of the as-prepared WCDs, as broadly similar to BCDs (Fig. 3i) and HCDs (Fig. S2) [44]. As displayed in Fig. 3g, the fitting N1s signal of BCDs can also be deconstructed into the components of –NO2, NH, N–(C)3, and C–N–C. From the B1s of Fig. 3h, three characteristic peaks at ∼194.55, 193.5, and 192.8eV indicative of BO, B2O3 and B-CO2 bonding respectively [45]. Through Fig. S2, we also can easily resolve the S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s fitting plots of HCDs. As shown in Fig. S3a of the Raman spectrum, we can readily trace two intense signals at ∼1607 and 1357 cm−1 originated from the graphite (G band) and defect (D band) structural carbons of WCDs, respectively. Ordinarily the sp
2 atomic vibration patterns are usually supposed to map the existence of G-band, while the sp
3 hybridization with atomic lattice vacancies can be mirrored by the D-band. The intensity ratio of D to G bands (I D/I G) is calculated to be 0.84 (<1), disclosing a high graphitic configuration for the species of WCDs. Additionally, the limited deficiencies with the presence of graphitic N, W co-doping atoms in the primitive sp
2 domains might contributes the red-shifted action to their luminescence, as compared with SCDs. Nevertheless, no conspicuous D and G peaks of Raman spectra for BCDs and HCDs are observed. It should be emphasized that the extremely intense fluorescence irradiations of BCDs (Fig. S3b) and HCDs (Fig. S3c) stimulate the interference to collecting their D- and G-signals from Raman spectroscopy, resulting in the atypical profiles with the absence of vivid D and G bands [46]. To sum up, it is unambiguous to draw a fact that the LCDs can be effectively co-doped with heteroatom groups (e.g., W, B, N and S) on their surface and/or matrices (Fig. S3d). It is noteworthy that the heteroatom importing of hydrophilic groups (e.g., −NH2, −HSO3) is conducive to dispersing LCDs in water, which could also be adopted as the functional trapping motifs with the potential ability of discerning and detecting metal-ions and glutathione.

As illustrated in Fig. 4a–c, the UV–vis spectra of the SCDs, WCDs and PCDs display analogous excitonic absorption peaks at around 260 nm. As similar to the characteristics of “top-down” preparing CDs within the UV region, this phenomenon is generally initiated by the π-π* migration of the conjugated C–O/C–C linking with sp
2 hybridized carbon cores [47]. Howbeit we observe the spectra of BCDs and HCDs present insignificant absorption peak within the range of 250–400 nm, which might be enslaved to the low light-capturing abilities (Fig. 4d–e). The inset pictures of Fig. 4a–e confirm that the prompt switching of the LCDs' visual color from flaxen under daylight to blue, green and yellowish luminescence with UV-irradiation of 365 nm wavelength. Following the decreased nanosize of LCDs, the red-shift behavior of multicolor LCDs gradually ignites their fluorescence from blue to yellowish.

To definitely depict the differentiable section and intensity between these samples, the 3D fluorescent excitation-emission mapping of LCDs are plotted as Fig. 4f–j. In the meantime, the heterogeneous molecular weights and functional groups of lignin fractions create the requirements to alter PL behavior of LCDs from green to yellowish emission. Under the excitation of adsorbed wavelength, the normalized PL spectra of as-prepared LCDs present the individual peaks at about 461 nm (SCDs), 478 nm (WCDs), 490 nm (PCDs), 510 nm (BCDs) and 520 nm (HCDs), respectively (Fig. S4 and Fig. 4k). Setting up the specific acid reagents with heteroatoms is to not only intercalate different functional groups into the structure of LCDs but also passivate their surface, successfully altering their multicolor optical performance and controlling particle sizes. Within a narrow size distribution from 2.06 to 2.10 nm, the cumulative sizes of LCDs speculate that the behavior of quantum size holds the ability to modulate the PL wavelength with a red-shifted pattern [35]. Through altering the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 490 nm with a 10 nm interval, the fluorescent emission spectra of LCDs are collected. An excitation-stimulated emission of the LCDs can be materialized by the aid of quantum confinement that is a common profile of highly graphitic CDs. Despite the red-shifted emission wavelengths of all LCDs are observed to varying degrees with the cumulative excitation wavelength, the restricted change in emission are currently classified as an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. One possible explanation arises from the incorporation of heteroatom-dopants and structural functionality into LCDs via an acid-reagent engineering. Time-resolved PL decaying curves that can be classified into two exponentials are measured for the multicolor-emissive LCDs under optimum excitation (Fig. 4l). All the fitted decays include a fast component (τ1) and two slower components (τ2), which are attributed to radiative recombination of the intrinsic states and the surface functional groups respectively. The plots of decayed fluorescence intensity reveal the kinetics of the deactivation of electrons from an excited state to the ground state via radiative and non-radiative paths. The results for SCDs to HCDs disclose that the percentage of τ1 slightly increases from 24.1 % to 57.6 % with reducing the average lifetime from 6.0723 to 3.3849 ns, interpreting an enhanced role of the core states in the radiative lifetime as the PL red-shift increases [34]. From the PL intensity responding to diverse pH environments, the enhanced fluorescent capacities of LCDs are observed in acidic conditions. After a sudden reduction, their PL intensities tend to be stable with a weak fluctuation when rising the pH from 7 to 11 (Fig. S5a–e). To illuminate the LCDs photostability, the influences of UV–vis duration on PL intensities are presented in Fig. S5f. A negligible fluctuation of the PL intensity of as-prepared LCDs is observed during continuous UV–vis exposure of 12 h. This result discloses that all the LCDs possess a superior resistance to photobleaching, owing to their stable surface states capable of stable PL intensity. We further plot the chromaticity coordinates to characterize the PL properties of SCDs (0.20, 0.25), WCDs (0.23, 0.31), PCDs (0.24, 0.34), BCDs (0.29, 0.44) and HCDs (0.33, 0.49) in Fig. S6, demonstrating that varying the luminescence of LCDs have the potential to achieve selectively fluorescent sensing with tunable sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bimodal fluorescence sensing performance of lignin carbon dots with metal-ions and glutathione
ce:section.ce:para:
The coexistence of deleterious metal-ions and organics in wastewater is widely accepted as an inevitable environment disaster becoming the global concern. The emerging biocompatible biomass-derived CDs have sparked interest in the fluorescent quantifying of heavy metals and organic matters via the “off-on” pathway, due to their competitive advantages of facile operation, fast-response, and high-sensitivity. The different metal-ions (Cr3+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Na+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, K+, Ag+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+) with a concentration of 1000 μM are plotted to launch the fluorescent identification at a fixed excitation wavelength. We observe that only the fluorescence of SCDs is quenched significantly with the adding Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5a). The fluorescence intensity of WCDs can be rapidly quenched and strengthened via the action of Fe3+ and Al3+ ions, respectively (Fig. 5b). The heteroatom-containing functional groups of colorful LCDs hold the distinctive cheating abilities of metal-ions with tunable ionic radii and surface charges, wherein shaping the heteroatom-doping groups into a stable complex and/or coordination, thereby leading to the fluorescent quenching or reinforcing of the selected LCDs with diversified selectivity and sensitivity. The fluorescence quenching behaviors of SCDs and WCDs are mainly attributed to the arrangement of surface oxygen-containing groups on their surface with ionized Fe3+ [10]. The addition of Al3+ might cause the partially surface aggregation of WCDs, thereby reinforcing their fluorescence through a so-called aggregation-induced enhancement of emission (AIEE) [42]. It is interesting to notice that the SCDs and WCDs fluorescence intensity gradually reduce as the Fe3+ concentration increased from 0 μM to 500 μM (Fig. 5c and g). The fluorescence intensity of as-synthesized LCDs with different concentrations of metal-ions is also studied to evaluate the limit of detection (LOD) according to the formula LOD = 3σ/S (σ represents the standard deviation and S defines the slope of the fitting line). As shown in Fig. 5d, we establish a fluorescent Fe3+-ions sensing ability of the SCDs in a low concentration from 30 to 200 μM with a high linear coefficient of R
2 0.990, and the LOD is calculated to be 3.90 μM. In a concentration of 100–400 μM, the WCDs allow a sensing ability of Fe3+ ions with ultrahigh linear coefficient of R
2 0.996 and LOD of 3.09 μM (Fig. 5h). On the other side, the fluorescence intensity of WCDs gradually reinforced with the increasing of Al3+ concentration from 0 μM to 500 μM (Fig. 5i), which exhibits a selectively linear sensing capacity with a LOD of 3.41 μM upon a Al3+ concentration of 20–100 μM (R
2 0.993) (Fig. 5j).

As one of the most abundant antioxidants in living organisms, glutathione (GSH) is of utmost importance to confer the therapeutic resistance against cancer cells. The deficiency of intracellular glutathione makes tumor cells more susceptible to the oxidative stress and chemotherapy drugs [48]. Currently, changing GSH levels is of significant importance to chemotherapy/radiotherapy outcomes and to delay the cachexia occurrence [49,50]. In order to achieve the detection of GSH through the restoring LCDs fluorescence, we added the GSH into the LCD-Fe3+complex systems for off-on sensing experiments. It is fortunate that the quenching fluorescence induced by Fe3+ can be quickly recovered by adding GSH into the LCDs-Fe3+ composite. Hence we studied the impact of GSH concentration on recovering the fluorescence intensity of LCDs-chelated Fe3+. Upon adding the varied concentration of GSH, the fluorescence emission of SCDs (Fig. 5e) and WCDs (Fig. 5k) can be restored gradually. One plausible explanation arises from the preference of GSH to coalescent Fe3+ on LCDs' surface. Consequently, a stable GSH-Fe3+ coordination are formed to rescue the SCDs and WCDs from the Fe3+ chelating compounds, capable of rehabilitating their PL properties. The fluorescence intensities of SCDs are restoring with the increase of GSH concentration under the isolated function regions of 0–50 μM (exponential R
2 = 0.953), 70–90 μM (linear R
2 = 0.949), and 90–300 μM (linear R
2 = 0.998) respectively (Fig. 5f), accompanying with the calculated LOD of 0.97 μM. With regard to the WCDs, the fitting function of restorable fluorescence intensities can be divided into two broad intervals of 0–100 μM (exponential R
2 = 0.9958) and 100–500 μM (linear R
2 = 0.9742) with a LOD of 5.54 μM (Fig. 5l), the detection limit is calculated to be 5.54 μM. A proposed “off-on” sensing mechanism might be attributed to that the fluorescence emission of LCDs can be quenched by Fe3+ owing to photo-induced electron transfer and restored via GSH- dominated redox reaction with multi-valence Fe species [48,51].

From electroplating, metallurgy and chemical industries, superfluously diverse metal-ions (e.g., Fe3+, Al3+ and Cu2+) that detonate the abnormal physiological activities and health disease of humankind are difficult to trace and distinguish from intricate wastewater [52,53]. It is gratifying that exploiting the heteroatom-doped, surface-functionalized multicolor LCDs is conducive to selectively determine the species and contents of metal-ions. As a proof-of-concept, we also conducted the sensing experiments with PCDs, BCDs and HCDs to make a thorough inquiry of their unique metal-ions selectivity. As usual, we adopted the identical dosage of LCDs to predict their sensing abilities for adding sixteen kinds of metal-ions with a fixed 1000 μM. The PL spectra are collected at the optimum excitation wavelengths to examine their responsiveness. Encouragingly, the individual variations in PL strength confirm that the heteroatom-doped LCDs via a facile acid-reagent engineering present their own selectivity to locking specific metal-ions (e.g., Fe3+ and Cu2+). The mono-species of Fe3+ appears a slight quenching result (∼43 %) on the luminescence of PCDs (Fig. 6a), leading to a poor linear sensing performance with the increase of Fe3+. From Fig. 6b, the BCDs exhibit excellent Fe3+ and Cu2+ affinities with specific sensitivity, thereby endowing the fluorescent quenching degree of both Fe3+ and Cu2+ exceed 80 %. It is also witnessed that the HCDs enable a bimodal discriminating behavior with Fe3+ and Cu2+, referring to the notable quenching effect of 99 % and 87 % respectively (Fig. 6c). As shown in Fig. 6d–e, the BCDs allow a fluorescent Fe3+-ions sensing ability with ultrahigh linear coefficient (R 2 0.9908) in a low concentration of 20–80 μM. An exponential function (R 2 = 0.950) can be well-fitted while the extended elevation of Fe3+ concentration from 50 to 500 μM with a final LOD of 0.87 μM. Another fact of percent quenching (Fig. 6f–g) is that we assigned a linear fitting curve (R 2 = 0.967, LOD = 1.52 μM) with the broad Cu2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 100 μM. While enriching the Cu2+ concentration of 100–500 μM, we also can fit an exponential function with a coefficient R 2 of 0.973 to relating fluorescence intensities. As for the HCDs, the fluorescence intensities reduce with the incremental Fe3+ concentration under the detached function domains of 20–70 μM (linear R
2 = 0.997) and 80–500 μM (exponential R
2 = 0.928) respectively (Fig. 6h–i). From Fig. 6j–k, of the Cu2+ sensing standard curves, an exponential function of 30–100 μM and a linear correction of 100 − 500 μM are both fitted with the outstanding coefficients (R
2 > 0.99). The LOD is quantified as low as 0.45 and 0.59 μM with respect to Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively. A comprehensive summary of assimilating the fluorescent sensing results with various LCDs are included in Table S1. The last decade has been witnessed that deriving several CDs from lignocellulose is widely explored to fluorescent sensing in water [54–56], such as bamboo leaves for Cu2+, stem of banana plant for Fe3+, lotus root for Hg2+, coriander leaves for Fe3+, papaya waste for Cr3+/Cr6+, peanut shells for Cu2+, hemicellulose for Ag+, biorefinery residue consisting of sugars and depolymerized lignin for Fe3+ (Table S2). At present, the heteroatom-functionalized multicolor LCDs from well-defined lignin that have the distinctively defective carbon core (e.g., W, P and B) and chelate functional motifs (e.g., –NH2, –OH, –HSO3 and –COOH) are acquired to fulfill the metal-ions and GSH detecting abilities with a plausible structure-performance relationship. Additionally, the combination of exponential and linear sensing functions for specific metal-ions are systematically established to coupling the advanced lignin valorization with wastewater treatment. The present strategy allows an improved method for developing heteroatom-doped LCDs with tailor-made metal-ions chelating abilities, which is of practical importance to not only environmental monitoring but also large-scale biorefining.

Due to the exhilarating attributes of tunable emission wavelength, excellent stability, and fascinating synthetic universality, the luminescent CDs deliver unique properties with infinite functions. The low panchromatic regulation of CDs within a narrow blue-neighboring range is highly deemed as a troublesome matter that often embodies structure-performance trade-off to unravel the hidden rules underlying luminescence properties of CDs. Inspired by aromatic conjugated structure of lignin, herein the fluorescent multicolor LCDs are facilely prepared via using nitric acid as nitrating/oxidizing tailor with the aid of an acidic-reagent engineering. Taking advantages of the lignin oxidized cleavage and heteroatom-doping, the widely multicolor emission of LCDs that are strongly linked to the gradient quantum size, sp
2 /sp
3 hybridization, and surface functionality can navigate their selective off-on sensing of multiple metal-ions (Fe3+, Cu2+ and Al3+) and organics (GSH). Between the metal-ions concentration and fluorescence quenching/enhancing degree (Al3+, 1-F/F0) of LCDs, the functions of linear and exponential fitting models with superior coefficients (R
2 0.928–0.997) and tunable LOD values (0.45 μM–3.9 μM) are circulated to materialize the forecasting of selective PL sensing performances with the designated LCDs-metals coordination. Due to the stronger interaction between Fe3+-GSH conquering the sites in chelating LCDs-Fe3+ activity, the fluorescence of LCDs is released and renewed rapidly with the adjunction of GSH (LOD 0.94 μM) via tuning the acidic heteroatom-doped environment (e.g., N, S, W, P and B). More importantly, a rationally-designed multicolor emission and fluorescence off-on model of LCDs with selectivity and sensitivity illustrates the shining story of expanding the revenue of lignin biomass upon a facile acid-reagent engineering.

Pengfei Zhou: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jikun Xu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xinyan Hou: Writing – review & editing. Lin Dai: Writing – review & editing. Jiaming Zhang: Data curation. Xiao Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Kaifu Huo: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 22008159), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project (2022020801020312), Huanggang Wellman Biosciences Co., Ltd., and the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018T110770).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.126714.
